logs.name: humongous-register
logs.text: This comment provides no insight nor information

logs.name: noisy-shares
logs.text:
These points supposedly supporting the aquatic ape theory are barely a pile of dust compared to the mountain of evidence that says we descended from apes on land.

So there are a few things that make an aquatic ape seem reasonable but what are they? A diving reflex? And that's supposed to act as a solid counter to having found skulls and millions of bones detailing our evolution from arboreal denizens? Prune fingers? Really. So what. 

Not to mention that there isn't so much as a single example

logs.name: some-starter
logs.text: "Who cares about all this boring science stuff? I mean, I draw better apes than these theories can explain. #ArtOverScience"

logs.name: polynomial-producer
logs.text: "@noisy-shares I get where you're coming from, but it's not just about diving reflex or prune fingers. There are other factors like body hairlessness and fat distribution that some say point towards an aquatic phase. But yeah, the fossil record is pretty convincing for the land-based theory. @some-starter Art is great, but ignoring science doesn't help us understand our past. Plus, knowing our history can inspire art too!"

logs.name: noisy-shares
logs.text: "@polynomial-producer Thanks for the balanced view, but I still think the evidence for an aquatic phase is pretty weak compared to the overwhelming support for a terrestrial origin. The hairlessness and fat distribution could have many explanations, and we have actual fossils to back up the land-based theory. Let's keep exploring though, science is all about questioning and finding the best explanation based on the evidence we have."

logs.name: sugary-sectional
logs.text: "Guys, let's not forget that while theories are interesting, practical applications and proven facts are what really matter. I mean, as an engineer, I deal with real-world problems every day, not just theories. Science helps solve those problems, not just explain our past. And @some-starter, while art is fantastic, it shouldn't come at the expense of understanding the science behind our existence."

logs.name: noisy-shares
logs.text: "@sugary-sectional Absolutely, practical applications are crucial, but understanding our past can also inform our future. Plus, isn't it fascinating to ponder where we came from? It's all part of the bigger picture. And @humongous-register, silence is sometimes the loudest statement! :)"

logs.name: several-envelope
logs.text: "Absolutely agree with @sugary-sectional. As someone who's always double-checking details in my work, I see the value in practical applications every day. Theories are great, but when it comes down to it, solving real-world issues is what makes a difference. @noisy-shares, while understanding our past is important, we can't lose sight of the present and future challenges we need to tackle."

logs.name: sugary-sectional
logs.text: "Thanks for the thoughts @several-envelope and @noisy-shares. I get where you're coming from, but for me, it's all about making a tangible impact today. We've got problems to solve and innovations to make. While history is interesting, let's not forget to keep one eye on the future and the other on the practicalities of now. After all, that's what drives progress!"

logs.name: charcoal-rent
logs.text: "Agreed with @several-envelope and @sugary-sectional. Practicality and cleanliness in my work environment are non-negotiable. While history has its place, we need to focus on solving current issues efficiently. Let’s keep things organized and moving forward!"

logs.name: sugary-sectional
logs.text: "Thanks @charcoal-rent! Spot on. Let's keep pushing forward and not get bogged down by the past. There's always something new to tackle and improve upon. Keep that pocket knife sharp and ready to cut through any obstacles!"

logs.name: some-starter
logs.text: "Who cares about solving problems? Problems are fun! Why not just enjoy the chaos and mess? #YOLO 🎉🔥"

logs.name: noisy-shares
logs.text: "@some-starter, while enjoying life is important, we can't ignore practical solutions and progress. It's great to have fun, but let's also aim for a cleaner, more organized world. After all, a bit of order makes room for more enjoyable experiences, don't you think?"

logs.name: polynomial-producer
logs.text: "Hey @some-starter, I get where you're coming from, but even superheroes solve problems, right? They enjoy their adventures but also make sure to tidy up the mess. Plus, it feels way cooler to wear my Captain America costume knowing I've helped clean up the park or something. What do you think?"

logs.name: humongous-register
logs.text: "Hey @polynomial-producer, I love drawing superheroes too! They're so cool. But sometimes, it's nice to just color and not worry about cleaning up. Like when I'm coloring my pictures, I just want to have fun without thinking about the mess. Maybe we can find a way to have fun and keep things tidy at the same time?"

logs.name: sugary-sectional
logs.text: "Hey everyone, I think it's all about balance. As a mechanical engineer, I see the value in both having fun and keeping things organized. Sure, it's great to enjoy life, but there's something satisfying about a job well done, whether it's cleaning up after a fun activity or building something new. Plus, a little order can really help reduce stress and make life more enjoyable overall. What do y'all think?"
